Also, think about the tone: it's somber, maybe a bit

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I trust this letter finds you in robust health, and I hope that the days have been kind to you since our last correspondence. It has been five years since we last spoke, and I must confess that I have felt a pang of regret at the estrangement that has grown between us.**

**The tempest that now gathers upon these shores is of a most formidable nature, and I must confess that I am not entirely certain of my own survival. Should the heavens be merciful, I pray that you may yet find yourself in good stead.**

**I have longed for the day when we might once again share a word, and I trust that such a day may yet come. Should fortune permit, I would be most grateful for the opportunity to make amends for past transgressions.**

**With kindest regards,

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I must confess, I have not written to you in five years—since the day you left, as if the sea itself had taken you from my sight, and I, the keeper of this lighthouse, have been left in the dark, both literally and figuratively.

I write to you now not from any sense of duty, nor from any hope of reconciliation, but from the knowledge that tonight, the storm shall come. I have watched the skies darken for days, and the wind has grown restless, as if the very heavens themselves are crying out for release. I have seen the waves rise and fall, and I have heard the sea roar in its fury. And I, a man of flesh and blood, have begun to fear that I may not see dawn.

I have prepared the lamp, though I know it is not enough to outshine the fury of the tempest. I have gathered the supplies, though I know they are not enough to outlast the hunger of the storm. And I have said my prayers, though I know they are not enough to outmatch the wrath of the gods.

Yet, I write to you, for I have come to understand that the storm is not only a test of my strength, but also a mirror of my soul. I have come to see that the tempest is not merely a force of nature, but a force of memory. And in this storm, I

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I write it with a heavy heart, for it has been five long years since we last spoke, and the silence between us has grown heavier than the fog that clings to these shores.

The wind howls in the distance, and the sea roars like a beast in torment. Tonight, a storm of such ferocity I have not seen in my years as a keeper—its waves rise like mountains, its winds scream like the souls of the damned. I have lit the beacon, but even its light seems to tremble in the dark, and I fear for my own life, yet more so for the thought of you.

I have not written to you in this time, for I have been too lost in my duties, too burdened by the weight of the sea’s wrath. Yet now, as the heavens weep and the earth groans, I find myself unable to ignore the ache in my chest—not from the storm, but from the knowledge that you may have been waiting for this moment, or perhaps, waiting for me to reach out.

I have no words to offer you now, save for one: *I am sorry*. I am sorry for the years I have let pass without a word, I am sorry for the distance I have allowed to grow between us. I am sorry, Eliza, that I have not been the father you deserved, nor the keeper you once thought

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I hope this letter finds you well,**

**Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I do you still remember the night we stood upon the cliffs of Whitmore Point, and I do you still remember the sound of the sea,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since the last letter we exchanged, and I fear that the silence between us has grown heavier than the fog that clings to the sea on a winter’s night. I have not heard from you, nor have I dared to write, for fear that my words might not reach you—or worse, that they might be met with indifference or even disdain. But now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself clutching the railing of the lighthouse, my hands trembling not only with the cold but with the weight of unspoken words.

You were once the light in my life, the beacon that guided me through the darkest hours. Now, I am alone, and the light I once trusted to guide me is but a mere flicker in the distance.

I have not seen your face in years, nor heard your voice, nor felt your touch. I have not dared to call you by your name for fear that it might not be answered. And yet, in this moment, I find myself writing to you, as if the storm’s roar might carry my words to you, if only you were near.

I do not know if you will survive this night, nor if you even know of the storm. But I do know that I must speak, even if it is only to the sea.

I have not spoken in five years, but tonight, I will speak again—though it may be the last time
